title: Making Education Affordable and Accessible Act of 2025
short_title: Making Education Affordable and Accessible Act of 2025
plain_summary: This bill creates a new program that gives money to colleges to work with high schools. The program helps high school students take college classes while they are still in high school. The money can pay for things like teacher training, books, and buses to get students to classes. The program focuses on helping students from low-income families, students in rural areas, and students whose parents did not go to college. This helps make college more affordable and easier to access for these students.
last_action_text: Read twice and referred to the Committee on Health, Education, Labor, and Pensions.

sections.title: Section 1 Short Title
sections.text: This law can be called the Making Education Affordable and Accessible Act of two thousand twenty-five.

sections.title: Section 2 Dual or Concurrent Enrollment Programs and Early College High School
sections.text:
This section changes an existing education law called the Higher Education Act of nineteen sixty-five. It moves one section to a new number and updates a date from two thousand nine to two thousand twenty-five. It also adds a new section seven hundred forty-five with the following parts.

Purpose. The goal is to give more high school students chances to take college classes while still in high school. These programs are partnerships between local school districts and colleges that let students earn college credits before graduating high school.

Definitions. Dual or concurrent enrollment program means a program where high school students take college-level courses for both high school and college credit. Early college high school means a high school designed so students can earn college credits or even an associate degree by the time they graduate. Eligible institution means a college that runs or plans to run these types of programs. First-generation college student means a student whose parents did not complete college. Rural school means a school located in a countryside area based on government location codes.

Grants Authorized. The Secretary of Education can give money grants to eligible colleges to run dual enrollment programs or early college high school programs. Each grant can last up to five years. The Secretary can renew a grant if the college shows good results based on required reports.

Application. Colleges that want grants must submit applications at the time and in the way the Secretary requires. The application must describe the partnership between the college and local school districts. It must also explain how the college will expand student access to these programs, especially for the priority students listed below.

Priority. When choosing which colleges get grants, the Secretary will give priority to colleges that serve students from low-income families, students who attend rural schools, or first-generation college students.

Use of Funds. Colleges that receive grants must use the money to run dual enrollment programs or early college high school programs for public high school students. They can use the money for training teachers and school leaders to provide college-level courses. They can design course sequences that match college standards and work with both high school teachers and college professors. They can create systems for approving courses that give both high school and college credit at two-year and four-year colleges in their state. They can create outreach programs to tell elementary and middle school students and their parents, teachers, counselors, and principals about these programs, including what students need to qualify and how to prepare. They can help students meet requirements for college courses and understand how credits will transfer to other colleges. They can coordinate high school schedules with college calendars.

Flexibility of Funds. Colleges can use grant money for any costs related to running these programs. This includes tuition and fees, books, and required materials so students do not have to pay for college courses. It also includes transportation to and from the programs. However, colleges cannot spend more than twenty percent of their grant money on transportation costs.

Evaluation and Reports. Each college receiving a grant must conduct an independent evaluation of how effective and rigorous their programs are. The evaluation must include data on how many students enroll in each course and how many college credits students earn through these programs that transfer to other colleges. Colleges must prepare and submit a report with the evaluation results to the Secretary. The Secretary can set additional requirements for these evaluations. No later than three years after this law is passed, and every two years after that, the Secretary must submit a summary report to Congress describing the findings from all college evaluations.
